coredata.dc:title: WO3/N-CDs photocatalyst with Z-scheme heterojunction for efficient tetracycline degradation under visible and near-infrared light
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Shihu
Tan, Peng
Meng, Nuo
Cao, Xuxin
Wang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Using visible and near-infrared (NIR) light as excitation sources to realize the photocatalytic degradation of environmental pollutants is a promising strategy, which will broaden the application of solar energy, but some challenges still exist. Herein, a novel direct Z-scheme tungsten trioxide/nitrogen doped carbon dots (WO3/N-CDs) heterojunction photocatalyst with remarkably enhanced light-absorption and broad-response photocatalytic activity is successfully constructed. The obtained WO3/N-CDs (CDs, 5 wt%) exhibit stronger absorption properties in the NIR region than in the ultraviolet region, and superior degradation efficiency (96.0% within 1 h) towards tetracycline (TC). The reaction rate constant of 5%WO3/N-CDs reaches 0.0428 min−1, is 15.3 and 7.5 times that of N-CDs and WO3 respectively. The outstanding photocatalytic activity is on account of the intimate Z-scheme heterojunction interfacial contact, which promotes the spatial separation of electron-hole pairs, as well as reduces the barrier of electron transfer. Through morphology orientation, preferential growth of (002) crystal plane is induced, resulting in the formation of hexagonal single crystal structure of WO3, which is conducive to the construction of Z-scheme heterojunction between N-CDs and WO3. According to the analysis of energy band position and free radicals, 1O2, ·O2 -, and h+ take part in the degradation process. Overall, this research might enlighten the reasonable construction of heterostructure between new carbon material and semiconductor photocatalyst to realize the utilization of broad-spectrum sunlight.

XRD patterns(a) and FTIR spectra(b) of WO3, N-CDs, WO3/N-CDs, and high-resolution XPS C 1 s(c), N 1 s(d), O 1 s(e), W 4 f(f) spectra of WO3/N-CDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using visible and near-infrared (NIR) light as excitation sources to realize the photocatalytic degradation of environmental pollutants is a promising strategy, which will broaden the application of solar energy, but some challenges still exist. Herein, a novel direct Z-scheme tungsten trioxide/nitrogen doped carbon dots (WO3/N-CDs) heterojunction photocatalyst with remarkably enhanced light-absorption and broad-response photocatalytic activity is successfully constructed. The obtained WO3/N-CDs (CDs, 5 wt%) exhibit stronger absorption properties in the NIR region than in the ultraviolet region, and superior degradation efficiency (96.0% within 1 h) towards tetracycline (TC). The reaction rate constant of 5%WO3/N-CDs reaches 0.0428 min−1, is 15.3 and 7.5 times that of N-CDs and WO3 respectively. The outstanding photocatalytic activity is on account of the intimate Z-scheme heterojunction interfacial contact, which promotes the spatial separation of electron-hole pairs, as well as reduces the barrier of electron transfer. Through morphology orientation, preferential growth of (002) crystal plane is induced, resulting in the formation of hexagonal single crystal structure of WO3, which is conducive to the construction of Z-scheme heterojunction between N-CDs and WO3. According to the analysis of energy band position and free radicals, 1O2, ·O2 -, and h+ take part in the degradation process. Overall, this research might enlighten the reasonable construction of heterostructure between new carbon material and semiconductor photocatalyst to realize the utilization of broad-spectrum sunlight.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new pollutant, antibiotics in the water environment and their removal have gained considerable attention [1]. Tetracycline antibiotic (TC) is a broad-spectrum antibiotic, which is widely used in medical treatment and prevention of human and animal diseases because of their low price and effective bactericidal performance. In addtion, TC has also been extensively applied as a growth promoter in aquaculture, animal husbandry and agricultural production, making it one of the most widely used antibiotics in various countries. Excessive use and incomplete treatment of TC will cause serious harm to human health and the environment [2]. Compared with biological and physical methods for TC treatment, photocatalysis in chemical method is considered as an effective environmental pollution control and energy conversion technology, which has the advantages of high catalytic efficiency, low energy consumption, strong stability, complete degradation and no secondary pollution, and has a broad application prospect. Making full use of sunlight for photocatalysis is an effective and practical method to alleviate the pressure of energy and environment. Semiconductor photocatalysts, such as TiO2, InVO4, Bi2WO6, Ag2O and WN have attracted widespread attention for their ability to photo-catalytically degrade organic pollutants in the environment [3–7]. Nevertheless, due to the rapid electron-hole recombination and inherent energy band position limitation, the catalytic efficiency of traditional single-component photocatalyst is usually low, failing to realize the utilization of near infrared (NIR) light source. Various solutions have been proposed to overcome the above problems. Among them, metal photocatalyst with low-work function, high carrier density and excellent conductivity is a promising choice to realize low energy excited photocatalysis.

Tungsten trioxide (WO3) is a transition metal compound with semiconductor characteristics, which is considered to be a potential candidate. Due to its narrow bandgap (2.5–3.0 eV) [8], high oxidative ability of holes at the valence band (VB) [9] and photo-corrosion resistance [10], WO3 has emerged in the field of photo-driven environmental remediation. Besides, WO3 and its composites have been successfully applied in advanced oxidation [11], photoelectrochemical water oxidation [12], photocatalysis [13] and ultraviolet (UV) photodetector [14]. Compared with other tungsten-based compounds, tungsten oxide has the most stable crystal structure, excellent recycling performance, harmless acidity and low cost. Despite the above advantages, the photocatalytic activity of WO3 is restricted by the high recombination rate of the surface charge carries, which affects their participation in the interface redox reactions [15]. Besides, the conduction band (CB) of WO3 is relatively low, which hinders the reaction of WO3 with electron acceptors, making the recombination of carriers easier, and then weakening the photooxidation ability. Therefore, a variety of methods have been tried to improve the catalytic capacity of WO3, such as heterojunction construction [16], doping [17] and morphology control [18]. Among them, the construction of heterojunction structure provides a promising improvement pathway, and the selection of complementary catalyst is the key to overcome the inherent defects of WO3.

Carbon dots (CDs) have gradually gained attention due to their photochemical properties, including wide two-photon absorption surface [19], excellent electron transfer/storage capacity [20], low toxicity, superior biocompatibility [21] and up-converted photoluminescence [22]. It has been reported that the introduction of CDs can improve photocatalyst activity to a large extent. Zhang et al. [23] synthesized CDs/TiO2 nanotube arrays, in which CDs served as a sensitizer to promote the photoelectrochemical hydrogen generation under visible light. Xia et al. [24] fabricated CDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials, which exhibited high degradation ability for several organic pollutants. This may be because CDs acted as the electron acceptor in the compound. Several functional groups on the surface of CDs allows them to nucleate with metal-based compounds [25], and then CDs and metal can be closely bonded, thus improved the stability of composite. Particularly, through surface modification, the visible light absorption of CDs can be efficiently enhanced by reducing the bandgap energy [26]. For instance, surface nitrogen doping can reduce the work function of CDs and generate more delocalized charge, which is more conducive to electron transport and further improve the photocatalytic performance. The surface functionalized CDs can effectively play their role as cocatalysts to realize the utilization of a broad-spectrum sunlight.

In order to play the interaction between various catalysts, researchers have constructed several heterojunctions, among which Z-scheme heterojunction tends to be a satisfactory strategy in the field of photocatalysis. The Z-scheme heterojunction can achieve interlacing between band gaps, which can change the electron transport path. Over the last few decades, researchers have constructed numerous Z-scheme system. For instance, ZnFe2O4/WO3 [27], g-C3N4/WO3 [28], TiO2/WO3 [29], CZS/CDs/BWO [30], In2S2/CDs/TiO2 [31] and CDs/Bi4O5Br2 [32] have been reported for organic pollutants degradation and photocatalytic hydrogen production. Although the single-component catalysts have a synergistic effect with each other through the connection of heterojunction, the heterojunction interface formed by large-size materials is not perfect and often has many defects, which make it easy for electrons and holes to recombine on these defects. CDs with small size could be uniformly dispersed on the surface of catalyst photocatalyst, reducing the occurrence of defects and greatly improving the photocatalytic efficiency. It has been reported that the photochemical properties of WO3 can be effectively modified by CDs. Sun et al. demonstrated that the internal electric field of CDs can induce reverse transfer of e-/h+ and improve the photodegradation efficiency of TiO2/WO3/CDs nanocomposites [33]. Zhang et al. found that CDs can enhance a synergistic effect of WO3/TiO2-NRs and achieve efficient BPA removal in simulated seawater [34]. However, these studies have not constructed the composite structure between CDs and WO3 from the perspective of heterojunction. Actually, the modified CDs may have a well-matched band arramgment with WO3, and drive superior photoinduced electron transfer through the heterojunction interface. As a result, the direct Z-scheme heterostructure between nitrogen-modified CDs and WO3 was constructed to enhance photocatalytic activity and broad-spectrum performance in this study.

Herein, a novel photocatalyst combining N doped CDs (N-CDs) with WO3 nano-sheets via direct Z-scheme heterojunction was synthesized. Different from the previously reported composite process, the growth of WO3 hexagonal single crystal nanostructure was promoted through morphology orientation in this study, which contributed to the formation of heterojunction between small-sized materials, and then enhanced the catalytic performance of composite photocatalyst in the NIR region. TEM，XRD, FTIR and XPS characterization demonstrated the combination between WO3 and N-CDs, and energy band calculation determined the direction of electron transfer between them. The light adsorption of as-prepared WO3/N-CDs was investigated, ensuring the utilization of broad solar spectrum (400–1100 nm). The effect of different ratios of N-CDs incorporated with WO3 on tetracycline (TC) photodegradation was evaluated. It was verified that the 5%WO3/N-CDs have the maximum degradation rate (0.0428 min−1) due to their excellent light absorption in the NIR region. Various electrochemical analyses also indicated the possible impact mechanism of N-CDs on photocatalysis of WO3 was that the formed heterojunction interface acted as a medium for electron transfer. This study proposes a constructive method to improve the photocatalytic potential with new carbon material, which has potential application prospects in low-cost solar energy utilization.

Ammonium tungstate((NH4)10W12O14·xH2O), tartaric acid and citric acid were supplied by Sanjiang Saireda Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Ethylenediamine (EDA), urea, tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), ethylene glycol and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were obtained from Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid, tertiary butanol (TBA), EDTA-2Na, p-benzoquinone were supplied by Tianjin Solomon Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). All the reagents were analytically pure and did not require further purification.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was used to determine the phase of prepared materials by using an Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). The main surface chemical elements were characterized by Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo, America). The microstructure and appearance were characterized on a Talos F200X G2 field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, Czech), aberration-corrected high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) and a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (JSM-7800 F, Japan). The energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) attached to the TEM was utilized to check the element distribution of C, N, O and W contained in the sample. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a TENSOR 37 FTIR spectrometer (BRUKER, Germany) using KBr pellets. Electrochemical characterizations were measured with an electrochemical workstation (CHI660, China). The UV–visible diffuse reflection spectrum (DRS) was recorded on a U4150 UV–Vis-NIR spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were performed utilizing a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime was obtained on a FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescent spectrometer (Edinburgh, England). The reactive oxidative species (ROS) were identified via an electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) of N-CDs, WO3 and WO3/N-CDs were measured using a multi-station extended automatic fast surface comparison instrument (ASAP 2460, USA) at desorption temperature and time of 573.15 K and 6 h, respectively. The ultra-performance liquid chromatography coupled with Electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (UPLC-ESI-MS, Waters OA-SPE Waters Xevo TQ-S) was used to observe the intermediates during the photo-degradation of TC.

Typically, 0.3 g (NH4)10W12O14·xH2O and 0.1 g DL-tartaric acid were added into 25 mL deionized water and stir with magnetic force until dissolved. Then 10 mL HCl aqueous solution (1 M) was added dropwise into the solution, during which the color of the solution would turn into yellow-green. After centrifuging, the obtained product was washed for 3 times and dried in an oven. After cooling, the green solid was crushed into powder using a mortar and then transferred into a muffle furnace. Keeping a constant heating rate, the powder was heated to 700 ℃ for 3 h, during which a flowing nitrogen atmosphere was kept.

CDs were synthesized via solvothermal method [35,36]. Briefly, citric acid and urea were dissolved into ethylene glycol and then were transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated. After filtration and dialysis purification, CDs powder was obtained by freeze drying. Then, 22.2 μL of anhydrous ethylenediamine (EDA) was added into 20 mL CDs standard dispersion solution (1 mg/mL) and stirred for 30 min. WO3 of different weights was added into the above solution (CDs content were 1%, 3%, 5%, 10% and 20%, respectively), and then the stirring was continued for 30 min, followed by ultrasonic dispersion for 15 min. After that, the suspension was introduced into a Teflon reactor and maintained at 120 ℃ for 8 h. Subsequently, the suspension was centrifuged (10,000 r/min, 10 min). The centrifuged solids were further purified follow the order of ethanol and deionized water respectively. Eventually, the as-prepared catalyst was obtained after drying.

To assess the activity of photocatalyst, TC was chosen as the pollutant and degradation experiments were performed under simulated illumination. In each experiment, WO3/N-CDs (20 mg) was dispersed into 50 mL TC solution (20 mg/L), then an adsorption reaction was conducted for 30 min in the absence of light. Subsequently, a xenon lamp (300 W) was turned on. An UV cutoff filter (λ ˂ 420 nm) was installed in front of each reaction vessel. The reactive temperature maintained at 25 ℃ by a combined circulating wind and water system. A sample was taken every 10 min and centrifuged. After filtering through 0.22 µm membrane, the absorbance of solution was recorded at 356 nm.

To determine the reactive free radicals that play a role in TC degradation, free radical scavenging experiments were carried out. Several scavengers were dropped into the reaction system. Specifically, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), isopropanol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) and L-histidine were chosen as the scavenger of h+, ·OH, ·O2 - and 1O2, respectively. Besides, the electron paramagnetic resonance spectra were collected to identify the reactive oxidative species.

Electrochemical characterization was carried out in a standard three-electrode system with Pt sheet as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode and indium tin oxide (ITO) glass coated with samples as the working electrode. The working electrode was made by coating the prepared catalyst onto the electrode sheet. Specifically, 5 mg sample was uniformly added into a mixture of 950 μL ethanol absolute 50 μL Nafion (5.0 wt%) and then dispersed 30 min under the assistance of ultrasound. Subsequently, the suspension was adhered onto the ITO glass as an electrode. The photocurrent response i-t curve was determined under a 0.2 V bias voltage for 250 s in 50 mM Na2SO4 aqueous solution.

The morphologic features of as-synthesized samples were investigated via HR-TEM. As depicted in Fig. 1 and Fig. S1, a monodisperse and uniformly distributed ultrathin coating is attached to the surface of WO3 nanosheets, suggesting that N-CDs was successfully coated on WO3 to form a heterogeneous structure. Fig. S2 and Fig. S3 is the TEM image of WO3 and SEM image of WO3/N-CDs respectively, displaying the regular nanosheets morphology. High-resolution TEM images of as-synthesized sample affirmed the lattice plane and microstructure of WO3/N-CDs. Fig. 1(a) showed that the measured lattice distance were 0.321 nm, 0.351 nm, and 0.380 nm, which were attributed to the (110), (100) and (200) planes of WO3, respectively. An amorphous structure of N-CDs was observed between the lattice streaks of WO3 on both sides. Clearly, high quality interfacial heterojunction contacts were formed between WO3 and N-CDs. Besides, the HAAFD-TEM elements mapping of WO3/N-CDs (Fig. 1b-g) further confirmed that the major elements were uniformly dispersed.

Fig. 2(a) is the XRD patterns of WO3, N-CDs, and WO3/N-CDs samples. Main diffraction peaks at 2θ of 23.4°, 33.9° and 42.1° correspond to the (200), (220) and (222) crystal face of WO3 [37,38]. The characteristic peak of amorphous carbon appears at 25.7°, which is possessed by CDs. For WO3/N-CDs sample, except for the peaks that can be found in the XRD patterns of N-CDs and WO3, several diffraction peaks between 2θ= 10–20° belonging to the (020) and (111) crystal planes of orthorhombic WO3 appear [39,40]. These diffraction peaks in WO3/N-CDs show that the incorporation of N-CDs results in the formation of some new crystal structures. Moreover, the characteristic peak attributing to N-CDs dropped significantly, which may be caused by the high dispersion of N-CDs on the surface of WO3 [41]. The absolute unit cell parameters of three materials are listed in Table 1. Compared with WO3, the crystallite size of WO3/N-CDs is obviously reduced to 5.8 nm. This may be because the doping of N-CDs increases the resistance of WO3 crystallite growth [40]. The lattice constant of WO3/N-CDs is about 2.4 times of that of WO3 and the micro-strain of WO3/N-CDs is higher, indicating that the incorporation of N-CDs into the crystal structure of WO3 induces greater tension in composite [42].

In the XRD pattern, the diffraction peak intensity of (200) crystal plane in WO3 and WO3/N-CDs is obviously higher than that of other peaks, which is different from other literature reports. The synthesized WO3 in other literatures is mainly composed of (020) crystal face. This may be due to the introduction of the form directing agent tartaric acid (TA) in the synthesis process, which changes the morphology of nanoparticles by promoting or inhibiting different crystal growth. TA is anisotropically adsorbed on the crystal planes, and its carboxyl and hydroxyl groups can coordinate with W ions to control the growth rate of various crystal planes of WO3, thus determining the product shape. In general, the existence of TA will cause WO3 to grow in a hexagonal single crystal structure, showing a nano-lamellar structure. Zheng et al. reported that WO3 with nano-lamellar structure has enhanced absorption for visible light, and the highly exposed (200) crystal plane has high photocurrent density, which can realize efficient photoelectrochemical water decomposition [43]. The use of morphology directing agent in this study has a significant influence on the structure and shape of WO3, and the preferential growth of (200) crystal plane induced by TA may improve the photocatalytic efficiency of WO3. In addition, compared with the WO3 system, the relative intensity of (200) peak in the WO3/N-CDs is higher, indicating that this crystal plane is more conducive to the formation of heterogeneous interfaces between WO3 and N-CDs.

FTIR spectra was applied to observe the functional groups on the surface of these samples. As illustrated in Fig. 2(b), WO3 and WO3/N-CDs exhibited similar vibration characteristics. The tensile vibration peak of WO appears at 960 cm−1 [44]. A wide range of absorption band from 1200 to 1600 cm−1is be associated with the aromatic CN heterocyclic ring [45], yet WO3 has no absorption peak in this region, which indicates the successfully combination of N-CDs and WO3.

XPS was applied to further analyze the interaction relationship. The full XPS analysis of WO3/N-CDs showed four peaks at 284.7, 400.1, 531.0 and 35.1 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s and W4f, respectively. The C1s energy peak (Fig. 2c) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.80 eV, 286.19 eV and 287.96 eV, which are assigned to the C-C/CC, C-N/C-O and CO [46]. The high resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) showed two peaks located at 400.15 eV and 401.79 eV, corresponding to N-H and C-N bond respectively [47]. The three peaks (Fig. 2e) at 530.19 eV, 531.25 eV and 532.47 eV can be ascribed to CO, W-O and C-OH/C-O-C bond [48], respectively. Compared with WO3, there is partial loss of oxygen in WO3/N-CDs. After deconvolution, the W 4 f spectrum of WO3/N-CDs was divided into four peaks (Fig. 2f), and combined into two pairs. The first pair of peaks at 35.52 eV and 37.69 eV belong to the W 4 f double crystal of hexavalent tungsten, corresponding to W6+f7/2 and W6+4 f5/2 respectively [49,50]. The other pair of peaks showed two acromia at 33.85 eV and 36.21 eV, which were not found in WO3 (Fig. S4). The two acromia arise from the W5+4 f7/2 and W5+4 f5/2 [51], which were derived from the partial reduction of W6+ caused by electron transfer accompanied by oxygen defect [52]. Literatures reported the W site near the oxygen defect of WO3 has higher adsorption energy and can transfer more electrons during the interaction with co-catalyst [53,54], which may be the active site for the photocatalytic reactions of composite catalyst. The binding energy of W5+ shifted negatively by about − 2.0 eV, indicating that WO3 has accepted electrons from CDs [55]. It is obvious that N-CDs have been successfully incorporated with WO3, which further proved the formation of the heterogeneous interface.

In order to characterize the specific surface area and porosity of the as-prepared samples, N2 adsorption measurement was carried out. Fig. S5 exhibit the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and corresponding pore size distribution of N-CDs, WO3 and WO3/N-CDs, respectively. According to Brunauer-Deming-Deming-Teller (BDDT) classification, both WO3 and WO3/N-CDs showed type Ⅲ N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms with H3 hysteresis loop, corresponding to the slit pores in nanosheet structure [56]. Table 2 shows the BET surface area, pore volume and BJH adsorption average pore diameter of the three samples. It can be found that the BET surface area of WO3/N-CDs (64.56 m²/g) is considerably higher than that of WO3 (22.76 m²/g), indicating that WO3/N-CDs have larger active surface and more active sites that can be used for photocatalytic reactions. Besides, the incorporation of N-CDs reduces the pore size of composite material, and the pore sizes of all three samples belong to the mesoporous range.

Diffuse reflection spectrum can reflect the response of photocatalyst to excitation light. Fig. 3(a) showed that both WO3 and WO3/N-CDs can absorb UV light in the range of 200–300 nm. For CDs, the adsorption peak may be caused by electron transport between the 2p orbital of N and the 2p orbital of C [57]. In contrast, WO3/N-CDs exhibited stronger and wider absorption, indicating an increase in the amount of photogenerated e-/h+ pairs. The absorption edge red-shifted from 405 nm to 435 nm, suggesting the combination has caused changes in optical absorption properties of WO3. Surprisingly, the light absorption performance of WO3/N-CDs in the near infrared region is significantly better than in the ultraviolet region, indicating that the synthesized photocatalysts have ideal wide range optical driving property. As illustrated in Fig. 3(b), the bandgap energy (Eg) of WO3 and WO3/N-CDs were calculated to be 2.96 eV and 2.79 eV (Text S1), respectively. The Eg of WO3/N-CDs decreases by about 0.17 eV compared with WO3, which makes WO3/N-CDs have a wider absorption range and achieve more efficient use of sunlight. Fig. 3c-d showed the valence band position of two catalysts. The VB of WO3/N-CDs (2.03 eV versus NHE) has shifted by 0.1 eV from that of WO3 (1.93 eV versus NHE).

The TC degradation ability of WO3/N-CDs under simulated sunlight radiation (wavelength: 400–1100 nm) was evaluated under different reaction conditions. TC was used as the target pollutant and a dark reaction was first performed before illumination. In a dark environment, the concentration of TC hardly changes after 30 min (Fig. S6), and the adsorption of TC by WO3/N-CDs is about 15%.

Firstly, the effect of N-CDs content in WO3/N-CDs heterojunction on catalytic performance was studied. When exposed to light alone for 60 min, almost no degradation of TC occurred ( Fig. 4a). In addition, WO3 or N-CDs showed low photocatalytic activity for TC degradation. When N-CDs were combined with WO3, the degradation efficiency of heterojunction system was obviously boosted, and the ratio of N-CDs has an effective impact on the degradation rate. Fig. 4(b) showed that the remove rate of TC gradually accelerated as the ratio of N-CDs increased from 1% to 5%. The degradation rate of 5%WO3/N-CDs (0.0428 min−1) system was 15.3 times that of N-CDs (0.0028 min−1) and 7.5 times that of WO3 (0.0057 min−1). However, when the CDs content was too high, the light shielding effect was aggravated [58]. N-CDs are materials with photoluminescence properties that can absorb the excitation light and emit fluorescence, which means the light directly irradiated on the surface of WO3 decreases as the N-CDs content increases, and more energy is lost in the form of fluorescence. That is, excessive N-CDs shield the light irradiated on the catalyst for photocatalytic reactions. As a result, the photocatalytic activities of 10% and 20% WO3/N-CDs declined. Optimally, 5% WO3/N-CDs were chosen for the following experiments.

It has been reported that pH plays a significant role in TC degradation [59]. Fig. 4(c) presented the effect of pH on TC removal. Under acidic and alkaline conditions, the removal efficiency of TC was relatively high, while it was lower in the near neutral range. TC is a typically amphoteric antibiotics, and mainly exists in the form of cation below pH 3.3, zwitterion between pH 3.3 and 7.7, and anion above pH 7.7 [60]. WO3/N-CDs had various charge properties at different pH. The isoelectric point of WO3/N-CDs is about 0.85 (Fig. S7), which means that WO3/N-CDs is negatively charged in solution above pH 0.5. When the pH is around 3, TC is positively charged, thus the electrostatic attraction between WO3/N-CDs and TC is strong, leading to the increase of removal rate. Adsorption is the basis of degradation process. TC can be adsorbed onto the catalyst surface in a large amount under acidic conditions, which is in favor of the rapid transfer of electrons between TC and WO3/CDs. TC has a self-degradation effect under alkaline conditions above pH 9, leading to a high TC removal rate [61,62].

To investigate the influence of WO3 structure on performance of composite catalysts, different heating temperatures (500 ℃, 600 ℃, 700 ℃, 800 ℃ and 900 ℃) were applied in the synthesis process of WO3. The WO3/N-CDs were then obtained by the hydrothermal reaction of WO3 with N-CDs, and the content of N-CDs in the composite catalysts at different temperatures is 5%. As shown in Fig. 4(d), the catalyst synthesized at 700 ℃ exhibited the best catalytic activity, which may be because the temperature has a great influence on the internal defects of tungsten metal during the heating process, and also changes the bonding mode between W, O and N [63].

In order to explore the charge separation of WO3/N-CDs, photoluminescence (PL) emission spectroscopy, time-resolved PL spectra, transient photocurrent response spectroscopy and EIS analysis were conducted. It has been reported that the PL intensity of catalyst is connected with its catalytic action [64]. Since the released energy can be converted into fluorescence, the more energy released by the photogenerated e-/h+ recombination, the stronger the fluorescence is. As a result, low fluorescence intensity tends to represent the low recombination rate [65]. Fig. 5(a) showed that the fluorescence emission peak of WO3 and WO3/N-CDs appeared at the same position, while the intensity in WO3/N-CDs system dropped sharply, which means that the involvement of N-CDs was conductive to separate the e- and h+ of WO3. Besides, the time-resolved PL spectra were also investigated (Fig. 5b). The average PL lifetimes of WO3 and WO3/N-CDs were calculated to be 3.14 ns and 4.94 ns through the fitted double exponential function [66]. From the perspective of charge separation dynamics, WO3/N-CDs have a slower exponential decay kinetics. Since the PL lifetime is inversely proportional to the electron transmission rate, a longer lifetime shows a higher transport rate of photocarriers, indicating that the barrier of electrons in WO3/N-CDs was weakened [67]. It can be considered that due to the tightly connected heterojunction interface between WO3 and N-CDs, a new electron transfer channel was established between them, thus changing the electron transfer path.

Higher photocurrent density represents higher separation efficiency of e-/h+ [68]. Fig. 5(c) illustrated that WO3/N-CDs system had higher photocurrent than WO3 system, implying that the separation efficiency of e-/h+ was enhanced remarkably due to the involvement of N-CDs, which is consistent with the PL results. Fig. 5(d) displayed the EIS Nyquist plots, the smaller semi-arc radius of WO3/N-CDs demonstrates a lower electron transfer resistance, indicating that the barrier of charge transfer is low, which makes it easier to transfer charge from samples to the pollutant. Based on the spectrum simulation of EIS results, the charge transfer resistances (Rct) of WO3 and WO3/N-CDs are obtained. Compared with WO3, the Rct of WO3/N-CDs decreases from 6.752 Ω to 5.706 Ω, showing that WO3/N-CDs have lower transmission resistance in the process of electron transfer, thus further accelerating the photocatalytic reaction rate. It is thus clear that the construction of Z-scheme heterojunction is conductive to the electron transfer, can prevent the e-/h+ recombination and further enhance the photocatalytic efficiency.

To identify the produced ROS during the reaction, p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), L-Histidine (L-His), EDTA-2Na and isopropanol (IPA) were used as scavengers for superoxide radicals(·O2), singlet oxygens (1O2), holes (h+) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH), respectively. Fig. 6(a) showed the removal efficiencies of TC by WO3/N-CDs with the addition of p-BQ, L-His and EDTA-2Na, hinting ·O2, 1O2 and h+ were the active species involved in the reaction during photocatalysis, which is in accordance with the band structure analysis of WO3/N-CDs. The CB potential (− 0.76 eV) of WO3/N-CDs is lower than the redox potential of O2/·O2 -, (− 0.33 eV), indicating that the photoexcited e- from CB can be trapped by O2 to generate·O2 - [69,70]. The VB potential (2.03 eV) of WO3/N-CDs is close to the redox potential of ·OH/OH-(1.99 eV), therefore ·OH is not easy to be generated. Fig. 6(b) illustrated that the scavengers successfully slowed down the decomposition of TC, which proved that ·O2 -, 1O2 and h+ were the radical that played a major role.

The EPR spin-trapping technique was utilized to further investigate the photogenerated ROS in WO3/N-CDs system and the corresponding consequences were displayed in Fig. 6(c-d). No peaks related to DMPO−·OH adduct were observed in the N-CDs, WO3 and WO3/N-CDs systems, indicating that ·OH did not participate in TC degradation. The signals of TBA−1O2 adduct were hardly detected in the N-CDs system, but were obvious in both WO3 and WO3/N-CDs systems. Moreover, the signal intensity in WO3/N-CDs system was higher than that in WO3 system, verifying more 1O2 radicals were generated in the heterojunction system.

It can be seen from Table 3 [71–80] that tungsten oxide can form Z-scheme or S-scheme heterojunction composite with other photocatalysts, and the heterojunction catalyst fabricated with N-CDs in this work displayed higher photocatalytic ability for TC degradation than most reported composite catalysts (especially those constructed with non-metal and WO3). Therefore, the fabricated WO3/N-CDs photocatalyst showed greater potential for treating TC.

Five repeated experiments confirmed that WO3/N-CDs photocatalyst was highly stable and recyclable. As shown in Fig. 7, although the mass of the catalyst was reduced in the process of filtration and collection, WO3/N-CDs still exhibited good photocatalytic activity.

In order to clearly elucidate the possible mechanism, E
CB and E
VB of photocatalyst were calculated. The VB positions were obtained by extrapolating the slope of the first XPS signal (Fig. S8，WO3 1.93 eV and N-CDs 1.64 eV versus NHE). The optical band gaps (E g) of the two samples were 2.96 eV and 2.97 eV versus NHE respectively according to the slopes of converted Tauc plot curves (Fig. S9) [81]. The theoretical band energy levels can be calculated by Eq. (1) [82]:
(1)
E CB = E VB – E g

Where E CB and E VB are the CB and VB energy potentials. Therefore, the calculated E CB of WO3 and N-CDs were − 1.03 eV and − 1.33 eV versus NHE, respectively. Because of the difference in Fermi level, the electrons transfer from N-CDs to WO3 after combining these two materials, thus WO3 is negatively charged while N-CDs are positively charged. Then, a built-in electric field (BIEF) is formed between N-CDs and WO3. The direction of BIEF points to WO3 [83].

Based on the above results, the potential photocatalytic mechanism of WO3/N-CDs was illustrated in Fig. 8. In this heterojunction system composed of WO3 and N-CDs, the close contact between them enables the formation Z-scheme photocatalytic system. Under light irradiation, WO3 and N-CDs in the heterojunctions are excited simultaneously to generate electron-hole pairs, and then the photo-induced electrons are injected into the empty orbit of CB. Meanwhile, the generated h+ remains in the VB. Subsequently, the BIEF facilitates the transfer of electrons in the CB of WO3 to the VB of N-CDs and the combination with h+ in the VB of N-CDs through the heterojunction interfaces between WO3 and N-CDs. Then, the photogenerated e+/h+ of WO3 are detached effectively and the recombination of e-/h+ pairs reduce dramatically. The pollutants attached to WO3 are directly decomposed by h+, which has transferred from VB to the surface of WO3. At the same time, the enriched electrons in the CB of N-CDs accelerate the generation of ·O2 -, which can be further oxidized to 1O2 by h+. As a result, ·O2 - and 1O2 act as the main radicals in the degradation of TC. The intense contact interfaces act as a high-speed channel for charges transportation, which greatly reduces the transportation resistance. The major reactions involved in the N-CDs/WO3 system are as follows:
(2) N-CDs/WO3 + hν (visible light) → N-CDs* + WO3* + h+ + e-
(3) WO3* + O2 + e- → WO3 + ·O2 -
(4)
·O2 - + h+ → ·OH2
(5)
·O2 - + ·OH2 → 1O2 + OH-
(6) (·O2 - + h+ + 1O2) + TC → byproducts
Direct heterojunction interface contact avoided electron mediators, and it is beneficial to avoid the recombination of e-/h+ pairs, thus promoting the redox ability of WO3/N-CDs system.

In order to clarify the degradation process of TC, ultra-performance liquid chromatography coupled with electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (UPLC-ESI-MS) was applied to identify the by-products of TC degradation. As shown in Fig. 9, TC degradation might occur via two pathways. In pathway Ⅰ, TC firstly generated P1 intermediate (m/z = 461) through hydroxylation reaction, and then undergone ring-opening reaction under the action of free radicals to generate P2 (m/z = 392) intermediate [84]. At the same time, the subsequent product P3 (m/z = 427) was formed via dehydration and dehydroxylation of P1 [85]. In pathway Ⅱ, TC first removed one molecule of water to obtain intermediate P3, and then one or two methyl groups in dimethylamine were removed successively to produce intermediates P4 (m/z = 411) and P5 (m/z = 399) [86]. With the progress of photocatalytic reaction, the above-mentioned intermediate products were further decomposed into small molecular substances, e.g. P6 (m/z = 319), P7 (m/z = 308), P8 (m/z = 291) and P9 (m/z = 251), and finally mineralized into CO2 and H2O.

In this research, a novel CDs and WO3 hybrid photocatalyst owing direct Z-scheme heterojunction was successfully synthesized via a simple process. The as-prepared WO3/N-CDs displayed significantly improved photocatalytic rate (0.0428 min−1) in TC decomposition under 400–1100 nm visible and NIR light in comparison with single N-CDs (0.0028 min−1) and WO3 (0.0057 min−1). The removal efficiency of TC by 5%WO3/N-CDs reached 96% within 60 min, and remained above 90% in five cycle experiments. This activity enhancement benefitted from the highly effective charge separation caused by the Z-scheme heterojunction and the intimate interfacial contact. The free radical quenching experiment and EPR test confirmed that 1O2, ·O2 -, and h+ played a major role in the degradation of TC. Through the identification of intermediates, it is speculated that the possible main degradation pathways of TC are hydroxylation and dehydration. In summary, this work confirmed that the full use of sunlight can be enhanced by designing and constructing the Z-scheme heterostructure between cocatalyst and semiconductor photocatalyst, which provides a creative solution for the utilization of visible and NIR light in the degradation of environmental pollutant. Although the use of near-infrared light has been realized, the power requirement of the light source in this study is still high, which may lead to large energy consumption. In addition, the performance of WO3/N-CDs in infrared region is still worthy of further study.
